params.text:
Keeping you protected

It is natural to worry about being identified. There is an important difference between the two words people often mix up:

  • Confidential — you give your name, but the people handling the concern keep your identity protected and share it only where they genuinely have to.
  • Anonymous — you give no name at all. This is allowed, but it is harder to investigate an anonymous concern or to give you feedback and support.

A gagging or confidentiality clause in a contract or settlement cannot be used to stop you making a protected disclosure — such clauses are unenforceable for that purpose. Wherever possible, raising a concern openly or confidentially (rather than anonymously) helps the concern to be dealt with properly.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Common worries about speaking up — and the reality.

dialogs.text: “If I speak up, everyone will know it was me.”
dialogs.answer: You can ask to raise your concern confidentially. Your identity is protected and shared only where genuinely necessary.

dialogs.text: “My contract says I can't tell anyone anything.”
dialogs.answer: A confidentiality clause cannot stop a protected disclosure — it is unenforceable for that purpose.

dialogs.text: “I'm not 100% sure, so I shouldn't say anything.”
dialogs.answer: You only need a reasonable belief. It is not your job to prove the wrongdoing — if in doubt, speak up.

dialogs.text: “I'll be punished for raising a concern.”
dialogs.answer: The law protects you from detriment and dismissal for making a protected disclosure.
